title: NOC 73301 – Bus drivers, subway operators and other transit operators | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 73301 professionals are the essential navigators of Canada’s urban and regional transit networks. As a TEER 3 occupation, this group includes drivers of urban transit buses, school buses, intercity coaches, and operators of subway trains, streetcars, and light rail transit (LRT) vehicles. In 2026, as Canada invests $15 billion in new transit infrastructure and shifts toward Zero-Emission Bus (ZEB) fleets, these operators are classified as Strategic Infrastructure Talent. For immigration, this role is a frequent target for the OINP In-Demand Skills Stream and the Express Entry Transport Category.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Level3Major Group73 – General tradesSub-Major Group733 – Transportation occupationsMinor Group7330 – Transportation occupationsNOC Code73301

duties: Route Navigation: Drive buses, streetcars, or rail vehicles to transport passengers along established routes to local or long-distance destinations.
Passenger Assistance: Drive wheelchair-accessible vehicles and provide manual or mechanical assistance to passengers during boarding and exiting.
Fare Management: Collect fares, issue and validate electronic or paper transfers, check passes, and record daily transactions.
Vehicle Inspection: Conduct mandatory pre-trip and post-trip inspections of vehicle systems, including brakes, lights, and safety equipment.
Communication: Maintain constant contact with dispatchers or control towers using two-way radio systems or on-board computers.
Safety Protocols: (Subway/LRT) Observe signals at crossings and arrival points; operate controls to open/close doors safely.
Emergency Management: Direct passengers during evacuation procedures and ensure welfare during technical malfunctions or accidents.
Student Safety: (School Bus) Monitor student activities to prevent distractions and ensure safety when boarding, leaving, or crossing streets.
Information Services: Provide passengers with accurate information regarding fares, schedules, stops, and points of interest for tours.
Reporting: Document and report delays, mechanical problems, and any incidents or accidents to the central control unit.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is typically required.

Licensing (Mandatory):Ontario: Requires a Class B, C, E, or F driver’s license depending on the vehicle type.Other Provinces: A Class 2 driver’s license is the standard requirement.Air Brake Endorsement (Z): Mandatory for all vehicles equipped with air brakes.
Ontario: Requires a Class B, C, E, or F driver’s license depending on the vehicle type.
Other Provinces: A Class 2 driver’s license is the standard requirement.
Air Brake Endorsement (Z): Mandatory for all vehicles equipped with air brakes.
Experience: A minimum of one year of safe driving experience is required. Transitioning to Subway or LRT roles usually requires prior experience as a public transit bus driver.
Training: Up to three months of specialized on-the-job training, including classroom instruction, is provided by the transit authority.
Certifications: A valid First Aid certificate is often required for safety-sensitive roles.
salary_prospects: Note: In 2026, experienced Subway and LRT Operators in major cities often earn annual salaries between $65,000 and $92,000 inclusive of shift premiums and overtime.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. While "Balance" is projected nationally, regional shortages remain acute in high-growth provinces. As Canada invests in the High-Frequency Rail (HFR) project and expands light rail in cities like Ottawa, Calgary, and Edmonton, the demand for specialized rail operators will outpace supply, ensuring long-term security.
labour_market_demand: The demand for transit operators in 2026 is High and Growing, particularly in major metropolitan hubs and rapidly expanding regional municipalities. In Ontario, the Waterloo Region and the GTA have emerged as high-demand zones, with OINP draws as recently as March 25, 2026, specifically targeting this NOC. In Western Canada, the federal and Yukon governments recently announced $7.8 million to expand Whitehorse's transit system through 2028. Nationally, "Replacement Demand" is the primary driver, as the industry seeks to replace over 67,000 workers by 2033 due to a significant wave of retirements.
Massive Fleet Electrification: Gain experience operating the latest electric and hydrogen-cell buses as Canada transitions to a 100% zero-emission transit goal.
Public Sector Stability: Many roles are within municipal transit authorities (e.g., TTC, OC Transpo, TransLink), offering superior job security and government-backed pensions.
Exceptional Benefits: Approximately 77% of workers in this NOC receive comprehensive non-wage benefits, including dental, medical, and life insurance.
Fast-Track PR Pathways: This NOC is a priority in 2026 Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP), with some provinces offering nominations with CRS scores as low as 32–40 in targeted draws.
pathways: Express Entry (Transport Category Selection): A high-priority NOC for 2026 category-based draws with lower CRS cut-offs.
OINP In-Demand Skills Stream: Specifically targeted this NOC in March 2026 draws for candidates already in Ontario.
BC PNP Skilled Worker: Priority for experienced drivers with a valid B.C. Class 2 license and a job offer.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-conversion route for drivers with a job offer from a designated transit employer in Atlantic Canada.
